title: Low breast milk and how to support it
excerpt: Low breast milk is more common than most new mothers are told, and Ayurveda has a quiet, nourishing answer rooted in a concept called stanya kshaya, a depletion of the milk-forming tissue that classical physicians mapped in careful detail two thousand years ago.
body_html:
when the milk feels like it isn't enough

Most new mothers know the quiet panic that sets in around day three or four: the baby seems unsettled after feeding, the breast feels less full, and the worry grows that something has gone wrong. Before reaching for a supplement or formula, it is worth understanding what Ayurveda says is actually happening in the body during lactation, because the classical texts offer a very practical framework.

The Ayurvedic term for breast milk is stanya, and it is considered one of the upadhatus, a secondary tissue formed from rasa dhatu, the primary nutrient fluid that circulates through the body after digestion. When milk production falls, classical physicians called it stanya kshaya, literally a diminishment of stanya. The Kashyapa Samhita, which is perhaps the most detailed ancient text on maternal and child health, devotes considerable attention to this condition and lists causes ranging from grief, exhaustion, and poor nourishment of the mother to inadequate warm fluids and suppression of natural urges (Kashyapa Samhita, Sutrasthana).

what you eat becomes rasa, and rasa, under the influence of the hormonal and doshic changes of the postpartum period, is channelled to the breast

what the classical texts say about how milk is made

Caraka Samhita explains that stanya is produced from rasa dhatu just as water rises from the earth, and that the same quality of lightness, sweetness, and nourishment that a mother takes in through food and drink directly shapes the quality of her milk (Caraka Samhita, Sutrasthana). This is not a metaphor. It is a statement about tissue physiology: what you eat becomes rasa, and rasa, under the influence of the hormonal and doshic changes of the postpartum period, is channelled to the breast.

Vata dosha plays a central role here. Vata governs movement and flow, and in the postpartum period it is naturally elevated because the body has just been through the intense downward effort of labour. If vata goes further out of balance through cold food, irregular meals, disrupted sleep, or anxiety, it can obstruct the channels that carry rasa to the breast tissue. Caraka Samhita describes these channels, the stanya vaha srotas, as having their roots in the breast and opening at the nipple (Caraka Samhita, Sharirasthana). Any obstruction in these channels, whether physical or caused by a disturbed dosha, can reduce the volume and quality of milk.

Sushruta Samhita adds a practical observation that is still useful today: a mother who does not feed frequently, or who is emotionally distressed, will experience a quicker decline in milk (Sushruta Samhita, Nidanasthana). The link between the mother's mental state and milk production is not new knowledge. Classical physicians understood it clearly.

everyday habits that support stanya

The classical galactagogue approach, what Ayurveda calls stanyajanana therapy, rests on three pillars: warm, unctuous, easy-to-digest food; specific herbs and foods known to nourish rasa dhatu; and reduction of vata-aggravating factors like cold, dryness, and irregular routine.

Warm milk is the simplest and most consistently recommended stanyajanana food across the Kashyapa Samhita and Caraka Samhita. A cup of full-fat milk, gently warmed and taken in the morning or before sleeping, directly nourishes rasa dhatu. If a mother tolerates it, a pinch of dry ginger in the milk aids absorption by kindling agni, the digestive fire, without adding heat in a way that disturbs the postpartum body.

Shatavari, Asparagus racemosus, is the herb the classical texts return to most often for supporting lactation. The Kashyapa Samhita explicitly names it in the context of stanya kshaya (Kashyapa Samhita, Chikitsa). It is typically taken as a fine powder, a quarter to half a teaspoon, stirred into warm milk. It has a sweet, cooling quality that helps counterbalance the typical postpartum vata elevation without aggravating pitta.

Beyond herbs, the daily rhythm matters enormously. The Caraka Samhita emphasises that a nursing mother should eat on time, rest adequately, and avoid suppressing the natural urges of hunger and thirst (Caraka Samhita, Sutrasthana). Skipping meals is among the more common reasons milk supply drops in practice, and it maps directly onto the classical understanding: starve rasa dhatu and stanya suffers. Eating warm, freshly cooked, moderately oily food, dal with ghee, khichdi, root vegetable soups, at regular intervals is both classical advice and straightforward nutrition.

Warm fluids throughout the day are equally important. Plain warm water, jeera water (cumin simmered in water), or a thin soup of fennel seeds in water are all traditional supports. These help maintain rasa and keep the stanya vaha srotas clear. Fennel in particular has a pleasant taste, suits most postpartum constitutions, and has been used across generations for exactly this purpose.

when to speak to a doctor or vaidya

This article is general information and is not a diagnosis or a substitute for medical care. If you are concerned about your milk supply, the first step is always to speak with a qualified lactation consultant or your doctor, because perceived low supply and actual low supply are different things, and most new mothers produce more than they think.

See a doctor or qualified vaidya promptly if: your newborn is not regaining birth weight by two weeks; your baby has fewer wet nappies than expected for their age; you notice pain, redness, or hardness in the breast that might suggest mastitis or a blocked duct; you have a fever in the postpartum period; or if you are taking any medication and want to add an herbal supplement like shatavari. Certain health conditions, including thyroid disorders and retained placental fragments, can cause genuinely low milk supply and require medical treatment, not dietary adjustment alone.

Try This Today

  • Shatavari in warm milk: Stir a quarter teaspoon of shatavari powder into a cup of warm full-fat milk. Drink once a day, morning or before bed. Consistent daily use over two to three weeks is more effective than occasional doses.
  • Jeera water through the day: Simmer half a teaspoon of cumin seeds in two cups of water for five minutes. Strain and sip warm through the day. It supports digestion, keeps the channels clear, and helps maintain hydration without bloating.
  • Fennel seed infusion: Add a teaspoon of fennel seeds to a cup of just-boiled water, cover and steep for ten minutes, then strain and drink. Fennel has a mild sweet taste that is pleasant during breastfeeding and is a traditional stanyajanana support.
  • Eat on time, every time: Skipping meals is one of the fastest ways to drop milk supply. Keep three warm, cooked meals fixed at regular times. Khichdi with ghee, dal and rice, or a thick vegetable soup with a roti are all ideal: easy on digestion, nourishing, and quick to prepare.
  • A pinch of dry ginger in food: A small pinch of dry ginger (saunth) added to warm milk or dal helps kindle digestive fire so that nutrients are actually absorbed into rasa dhatu. Avoid large amounts, which can be too heating in the postpartum period.

Key Ayurvedic Ingredients

Dry Ginger (सोंठ)

Used in small amounts to support agni and improve absorption of rasa-nourishing foods without excess heat.

Fennel (सौंफ)

A gentle, sweet-tasting galactagogue support used across generations to keep the milk channels clear.

Cumin (जीरा)

Brewed as jeera water to aid digestion, maintain hydration, and support the flow of rasa through the body.
author: Kerala Ayurveda Editorial Team

metafields.dek: Low breast milk is more common than most new mothers are told, and Ayurveda has a quiet, nourishing answer rooted in a concept called stanya kshaya, a depletion of the milk-forming tissue that classical physicians mapped in careful detail two thousand years ago.
metafields.disclaimer: Ayurveda guidance on AyurHQ is supportive care, not emergency or diagnostic medical treatment.
at_a_glance: Stanya kshaya, low milk production, is traced to depletion of rasa dhatu and obstruction of the stanya vaha srotas (milk channels).
Warm milk, shatavari, and regular warm meals are the core classical supports for increasing stanya.
A mother's emotional state and meal regularity directly affect milk supply, a fact the classical texts note plainly.

glossary_terms.definition: Breast milk; one of the secondary tissues (upadhatu) formed from rasa dhatu in the postpartum body.
Diminishment or depletion of breast milk; the classical term for low milk supply.
Galactagogue; herbs, foods, or therapies that increase the production of breast milk.
The primary nutrient fluid or plasma tissue; the foundation from which breast milk is formed according to Ayurvedic physiology.
The channels in the body that carry and deliver breast milk; rooted in the breast tissue and opening at the nipple.
Digestive fire; the capacity to transform food into nourishing tissue. Strong agni is essential for healthy rasa and, by extension, healthy milk.

sources: Kashyapa Samhita, Sutrasthana (causes of stanya kshaya including grief, exhaustion, poor nourishment, and suppression of natural urges)
Kashyapa Samhita, Chikitsasthana (stanya kshaya treatment and stanyajanana herbs including shatavari)
Caraka Samhita, Sutrasthana and Chikitsa (rasa dhatu as the origin of stanya; stanyajanana foods and dinacharya guidance for nursing mothers)
Caraka Samhita, Sharirasthana (stanya vaha srotas: roots in the breast, openings at the nipple)
Sushruta Samhita, Sharirasthana and Uttaratantra (emotional distress and infrequent feeding as causes of stanya kshaya)

faqs.question: What is the meaning of "Stanya" in Ayurveda?
faqs.answer: In the Ayurvedic tradition, Stanya is defined as: Breast milk; one of the secondary tissues (upadhatu) formed from rasa dhatu in the postpartum body.

faqs.question: How can I start addressing this issue today?
faqs.answer: One of the most recommended practices is to shatavari in warm milk. Stir a quarter teaspoon of shatavari powder into a cup of warm full-fat milk. Drink once a day, morning or before bed. Consistent daily use over two to three weeks is more effective than occasional doses.

faqs.question: What is the benefit of Dry Ginger for this condition?
faqs.answer: According to classical texts, Dry Ginger is highly regarded. Used in small amounts to support agni and improve absorption of rasa-nourishing foods without excess heat.
